stories_covered: Benchmark hires Everett Randall as new partner
Revolut $75B Series F at massive oversubscription
Vertical AI companies face TAM exhaustion
Poolside announces $2 gigawatt data center build
Microsoft reduces OpenAI commitments, Oracle steps in
harry_takes: Vertical AI plays are dangerously over-romanticized and will hit TAM exhaustion faster than horizontal solutions
Everyone being in-market simultaneously for AI tools is a temporary phenomenon; they won't repurchase annually after decision is made
The market has become compressed into a two-phase game: investing in mega-winners or investing in ultra-late-stage quasi-public companies
Founders have only 18 months to prove TAM expansion or risk becoming stuck in a niche with no path to venture scale
harry_boldest_quote: The easiest way to make money in 2025 is to take the very biggest companies and double down one more time.
predictions: Lemkin: TAM exhaustion is visible across his entire portfolio, wasn't even thinking about it 12 months ago
Panel: If AI CapEx train slows even 20%, valuations of vertical SaaS will crater because growth rates and TAM will disappear
Lemkin: Legal AI market will see initial buying surge followed by years where nobody repurchases because switching costs are high
Panel: Between Poolside building own data centers and capital intensity rising, only established AI winners can compete
disagreements: Lemkin skeptical that small-niche TAMs can expand into billion-dollar businesses; Stebbings and O'Driscoll argue best founders deliberately start in small TAM then expand. Lemkin remains unconvinced founders can overcome market saturation.
harry_role: Driving the thesis about TAM exhaustion and vertical AI risk, moderating between Lemkin's pessimism and O'Driscoll's more optimistic founder-centric view. Stebbings asks clarifying questions but takes a clear position on market consolidation toward mega-winners.
notable: Discussion highlights alarming capital intensity escalation: Poolside building own data center instead of leasing suggests AI infrastructure is becoming prohibitively expensive, potentially locking out new competitors.
